cbor.description: Signable canonical CBOR (RFC 8949) - pure loft. Encode with canonical map-key ordering + full decode (int/negint/bytes/text/array/map/bool/null); encode->decode->encode byte-identical, native == wasm.

crypto.description: Cryptographic primitives — SHA-256, HMAC, base64; Ed25519, X25519, HPKE (RFC 9180), ChaCha20-Poly1305 / XChaCha20-Poly1305 / AES-256-GCM, HKDF, OS CSPRNG.

arguments.description: CLI argument parsing — positional args + flags + auto-generated --help.

shapes.description: 2D geometry primitives — Rect, Circle, overlap tests, clamp.  Pure-loft, no other deps.

gridmesh.description: Chunk-local mesh-generation primitives for world-building algorithms.

api.doc:
A coord→cell-index reference, stored in the spatial-index hash keyed on
the encoded coordinate `ck`.

api.sig: pub fn enc_coord(x: integer, y: integer) -> integer
api.doc:
Encode an offset coord into one integer key.  The hex world is small;
±2^20 per axis covers it with room to spare and stays within i64.

api.sig: pub fn build_index(xs: vector<integer>, ys: vector<integer>) -> hash<CellRef[ck]>
api.doc:
Build the coord→cell-index spatial index for a placement-ordered cell
list given as parallel (xs, ys).  O(N), built once and reusable across
neighbour/on-axis queries.  (@P300 — assigning a hash-returning call's
result to a local — was fixed 2026-05-21, so this helper replaces the
old inline-build workaround the consumers used.)

Axial unit-direction components for hex axis d (0..5), via 60°·d.
Branch form avoids per-call vector-literal allocation.

api.sig: pub fn step_x(x: integer, y: integer, d: integer, k: integer) -> integer
api.doc: Offset coord `k` steps from (x, y) along hex axis `d`.

api.sig: pub fn step_y(_x: integer, y: integer, d: integer, k: integer) -> integer
pub fn idx_at(cidx: hash<CellRef[ck]>, x: integer, y: integer) -> integer
api.doc:
snap index of the cell at (x, y), or -1 if none.  (`cidx` not `index`
— `index` is a reserved keyed-collection-type keyword in loft.)

api.sig: pub fn nbr_count_idx(cidx: hash<CellRef[ck]>, cx: integer, cy: integer) -> integer
api.doc: Index-backed filled-neighbour count (distance 1) — the 6 axis-1 cells.

── B1: SegMesh accumulator ──────────────────────────────────────────

Struct-of-arrays line-segment mesh for segment/ribbon consumers (the
crystal rule).  Each segment is a 3D line (x0,y0,z0)→(x1,y1,z1) with a
kind, a colour, and the OWNING cell index (`cell_ix`) — the latter lets
a consumer attribute a segment back to its cell (e.g. GPU bloom-from-
centre keyed by cell age) and lets the dirty-rebuild find-and-replace a
cell's segments.  Coordinates are `single` (f32 — the GPU VBO format,
no precision loss vs what is rendered) and kind/colour are `u8`
(M1 narrowing: ~60 % less memory than the i64/f64 prototype).

Append one segment owned by cell `owner`.  Callers pass `float` coords
and `integer` kind/colour; they are narrowed to the storage width here.
Number of segments in the mesh.

Append all of `src`'s segments onto `dst` (concatenate the nine parallel
arrays).  Used to assemble a render-group mesh from its chunks' CACHED
meshes and to concatenate chunk meshes into a full build — order-preserving,
so the consumer can rely on a stable per-group draw order.

A chunk-space coordinate.  `ck = enc_coord(cx, cy)` is its identity for
dedup in the dirty index.

api.sig: pub fn chunk_of(x: integer, y: integer, shift: integer) -> ChunkKey
api.doc:
The chunk owning offset coord (x, y) for a field with `shift`
(chunk = 1<<shift cells per axis; shift 5 = the moros 32×32 chunk).

api.sig: pub fn field_new(layout: integer, xs: vector<integer>, ys: vector<integer>,
api.doc:
Build a field from placement-ordered coords.  The dirty index starts
empty; the spatial index (cidx) and the chunk->cell partition (buckets)
are built once (O(N)) and maintained incrementally thereafter
(field_add_cell), so a later collect_dirty_inputs is O(dirty), not O(N).

api.sig: pub fn field_add_cell(f: &ChunkField, x: integer, y: integer)
api.doc:
Edit — append a new cell (its index = old len(xs)), add it to its chunk's
bucket (O(1) amortised), and mark its chunk (+ halo-border neighbours)
dirty.  Keeping the bucket current here is what makes collect_dirty_inputs
O(dirty) rather than a full re-partition.

api.sig: pub fn field_mark_dirty(f: &ChunkField, x: integer, y: integer)
api.doc:
Edit — RECOLOUR an existing cell: mark its chunk (+ halo border) dirty
without touching the coord arrays or index (the cell stays; only its
consumer-side payload — colour/height — changed).  Erase uses
field_remove_cell instead (it must also drop the cell from the index).

api.sig: pub fn field_remove_cell(f: &ChunkField, x: integer, y: integer)
api.doc:
Edit — REMOVE an existing cell: drop it from the spatial index (cidx) and
its chunk bucket, then mark its chunk (+ halo border) dirty.  The MIRROR of
field_add_cell, sharing mark_borders so a removal flows through the IDENTICAL
incremental rebuild path as a paint.  The coord slot in xs/ys is deliberately
LEFT in place (not compacted): cell indices the field already handed out stay
stable, so a consumer's parallel per-cell arrays (e.g. the projector's bloom
births, indexed by cell index) are not invalidated.  The orphaned slot is
never read again — its index is in no bucket and no longer in cidx — and
re-painting the same coord allocates a FRESH index (field_add_cell appends),
so identity is never reused.
Clear the dirty index after a rebuild (@P307 OpClearKeyed: in-place free).
Number of chunks currently dirty (verification / O(dirty) sizing).

api.sig: pub fn chunk_is_dirty(f: ChunkField, x: integer, y: integer) -> boolean
api.doc: Is the chunk owning (x, y) currently marked dirty?

── B3: ChunkInput + per-chunk gather ─────────────────────────────────

What a per-cell RULE needs for ONE chunk: the indices of the cells it
OWNS (emit geometry for these) + the ≤halo_k border-neighbour cells in
OTHER chunks it may READ as context.  COMPACT — no payload, and NO copy
of the shared spatial index (`cidx`).  The rule receives `cidx`
SEPARATELY as a read-only ref (keyed params don't deep-copy) and `par`
forwards it as a context arg, so the per-chunk working set stays small
and cache-resident (copying the whole index per chunk would be O(N)).

api.sig: pub fn all_inputs(f: ChunkField, halo_k: integer) -> vector<ChunkInput>
api.doc:
One ChunkInput per chunk that holds cells (the full-build work-list).
Reads the wired-in `f.buckets` (no re-partition).

api.sig: pub fn collect_dirty_inputs(f: ChunkField, halo_k: integer) -> vector<ChunkInput>
api.doc:
One ChunkInput per DIRTY chunk (the incremental-rebuild work-list).
Iterates ONLY the dirty set and looks up each dirty chunk's bucket — so
the cost is O(dirty chunks × their cells), independent of total N.  A
dirty chunk with no owned cells (a halo-marked empty neighbour) is
skipped.  Clear the dirty index after rebuilding.

The chunk ck's a group contains that actually hold cells (the assemble
work-list).  No mesh here — gridmesh partitions; the consumer owns meshes.

api.sig: pub fn group_of(cx: integer, cy: integer, group_dim: integer) -> GroupKey
api.doc:
The group owning CHUNK coord (cx, cy): chunk-space divided by group_dim.
Uses chunk_div so any group_dim works (not just powers of two) — a free dial.

api.sig: pub fn all_groups(f: ChunkField, group_dim: integer) -> vector<GroupInput>
api.doc:
One GroupInput per group that holds cells (the full-build work-list).
O(chunks): each non-empty chunk dedups into its group's chunk list.

api.sig: pub fn collect_dirty_groups(f: ChunkField, group_dim: integer) -> vector<GroupInput>
api.doc:
One GroupInput per DIRTY group (the incremental re-upload work-list).
A group is dirty if any of its chunks is dirty; its chunk_cks lists ALL its
member chunks that hold cells (so the consumer reassembles the group's VBO
from those chunks' CACHED meshes — unchanged chunks copied, not rebuilt).

FLAT COST: derives the dirty groups from f.dirty (O(dirty)), then for each
enumerates its group_dim × group_dim member chunk coords analytically and
probes f.buckets — O(dirty groups × group_dim²), independent of total N.
(Never scans all chunks, which would make per-edit cost grow with N.)

graphics.description: 2D canvas + 3D rendering for loft (Canvas pixel surface, Mesh / Scene / glTF, OpenGL bindings).

imaging.description: PNG load/save + pixel manipulation for loft (Stage A — interpreter + native; wasm bridge deferred pending loft-host-ffi crate).

mesh3d.description: 3D geometry primitives — Vec/Mat, Mesh, Scene, Camera, Light.  Standalone (pure-loft, no GPU).

glb.description: glTF 2.0 binary (.glb) writer — exports Mesh / Scene as a glb file readable by Blender, three.js, gltf-validator.  Standalone (pure-loft, no GPU).

web.description: HTTP client + WebSocket client — ureq + tungstenite via cdylib.

api.doc: The result of an HTTP request.
Returns true when the status code is in the 200-299 range.
Send a GET request.

api.sig: pub fn http_post(url: text, body: text) -> HttpResponse
api.doc: Send a POST request with a body.

api.sig: pub fn http_put(url: text, body: text) -> HttpResponse
api.doc: Send a PUT request with a body.
Send a DELETE request.

api.sig: pub fn http_get_h(url: text, headers: vector<text>) -> HttpResponse
api.doc: GET with custom headers.

api.sig: pub fn http_post_h(url: text, body: text, headers: vector<text>) -> HttpResponse
api.doc: POST with custom headers.

api.sig: pub fn http_put_h(url: text, body: text, headers: vector<text>) -> HttpResponse
api.doc: PUT with custom headers.

api.sig: pub fn http_delete_h(url: text, headers: vector<text>) -> HttpResponse
api.doc: DELETE with custom headers.

Open a WebSocket session.  Returns null only if the URL is
malformed; transient connection failures (server not yet up,
network blip) are absorbed by the auto-reconnect loop in the Rust
layer, so this call always returns a usable handle for any
well-formed `ws://...` URL.

api.sig: pub fn send(self: WsHandler, msg: text) -> boolean
api.doc:
Send a text message to the server.  Returns true on success; false
means the connection is currently in backoff / reconnecting and the
caller may retry after the next `pump()`.

api.sig: pub fn send_binary(self: WsHandler, msg: text) -> boolean
api.doc:
Send `msg` as a binary WebSocket frame (opcode 2).  Loft `text`
is a byte buffer — pack u8/u16/i32/etc. into it via `to_bytes`
style code, then ship it.  Returns true on success; false means
the connection is currently in backoff / reconnecting.

Opcode of the last frame surfaced by `try_recv` / `pump` on this
handler.  Use after a successful read to decide whether to
interpret the payload as utf-8 text or as a binary blob.

api.sig: pub fn pump(self: WsHandler, on_message: fn(text)) -> integer
api.doc:
Drain any pending received messages, firing `on_message` for each.
Returns the count of messages delivered this call.  Call this from
a main loop (and yield to the host between iterations on the browser
target) to drive event delivery without async.

Non-blocking single-message receive.  Returns the next pending
message text if one is available, null otherwise.  Equivalent to a
single iteration of pump without the callback — useful when the
receiver wants to drive its own loop and accumulate messages
locally without capturing state inside a closure.
Permanently close the session (no further reconnect attempts).

Block the calling thread for `ms` milliseconds.  Convenience for
tests and pacing loops; non-positive values are a no-op.  Not
available on the WASM target — the host event loop is the
equivalent.
Empty the per-thread pack buffer.  Call before each blob.
Append a single byte to the pack buffer.  `b` is masked to 8 bits.
Append a 2-byte little-endian unsigned value.

Append a 4-byte little-endian unsigned value.  Loft `integer` is
i32 — bit-cast to u32 to preserve the LE byte pattern across the
sign boundary.

Take the contents of the pack buffer and return them as a `text`
whose UTF-8 bytes are exactly the buffer bytes.  Resets the buffer.

api.sig: pub fn byte_at(idx: integer, t: text) -> integer
api.doc:
Read the byte at `idx` of the text payload.  Out-of-range (negative
or past the end) returns -1.  Use to decode binary frames received
via `try_recv` / `pump`.  Argument order is (idx, t) — the C-ABI
auto-marshal in src/extensions.rs supports (I32, Text) -> I32 but
not (Text, I32) -> I32.
Create a new (empty) group.

api.sig: pub fn add(self: WsGroup, h: WsHandler)
api.doc: Add a handler to the group.

Poll all handles round-robin.  Returns a WsGroupEvent with
handle >= 0 and the message if one is ready; handle == -1
means nothing is available right now.

Yield one frame to the host event loop (@PLN84 ZT-C); native no-op,
browser asyncify suspend (loft_web.ws_yield).  Call once per WS poll
iteration so the browser event loop can deliver WebSocket messages.

server.description: HTTP + WebSocket server — TCP sockets + tungstenite via cdylib.

api.doc:
Non-blocking variant of `next()`.  Returns null immediately if no
connection is pending.  Use this when the server interleaves HTTP
accept with multi-client WS polling on a single port (single_port
audience demo / future TTT-style multi-tab apps).

api.sig: pub fn respond(self: Request, status: integer, body: text)
pub fn respond_typed(self: Request, status: integer, body: text, content_type: text)
pub fn respond_html(self: Request, body: text)
api.doc: Convenience wrappers for the common cases TTT v3 needs.

api.sig: pub fn respond_css(self: Request, body: text)
pub fn respond_js(self: Request, body: text)
pub fn respond_loft(self: Request, body: text)
pub fn send(self: WebSocket, msg: text) -> boolean
pub fn send_binary(self: WebSocket, msg: text) -> boolean
api.doc:
Send `msg` as a binary WebSocket frame (opcode 2).  Loft
`text` is a byte buffer — pack u8/u16/i32/etc. into it via
`to_bytes` style code, then ship it.

Opcode of the last frame returned by `next` on this socket.
Use after `next` to decide whether to interpret the payload
as utf-8 text or as a binary blob.

Event delivered to the on_event handler.  `connected` and
`message` are mutually-exclusive booleans for fast dispatch
without an integer enum.  Wire format `<msg_id>:<payload>`
is parsed by `run` so msg_id and payload come pre-split.

api.sig: pub fn run(self: Server, on_event: fn(WsEvent))
api.doc:
Block forever, draining events and calling the handler.
Malformed message frames are logged at warn level and silently
dropped — the handler never sees them.

Non-blocking single-event drain — returns the next WsEvent, or null
when nothing is pending.  Lets a server that also needs a periodic
tick (timers, decay, snapshots) run its OWN loop while still using the
efficient Rust-side event pump: drain `poll_event` in a batch, do the
periodic work, idle-sleep when a pass produced nothing.  `run` above is
the convenience wrapper for servers that only react to events.

Disconnect (kind 2) and malformed message frames are skipped internally
(the caller never sees them), so a null return always means "idle".

api.sig: pub fn respond_html(self: Server, body: text)
api.doc:
Reply to the HTTP request currently parked by a `http` WsEvent and
close it.  Call exactly once from the handler for an `ev.http` event.

api.sig: pub fn respond_typed(self: Server, status: integer, body: text, content_type: text)
pub fn send_to(self: Server, client_id: integer, msg: text) -> boolean
api.doc: Send a frame to a specific client.  Returns true on success.

api.sig: pub fn broadcast(self: Server, msg: text) -> integer
api.doc:
Send a frame to every active client.  Returns the count of
successful sends.  Iteration happens entirely in Rust.

api.sig: pub fn disconnect(self: Server, client_id: integer)
api.doc:
Forcefully close one client's connection.  The slot is freed
for reuse by future accepts.

Non-blocking variant of `next()`.  Returns null immediately if no
connection is pending.  Use this when the server interleaves HTTP
accept with multi-client WS polling on a single port (single_port
audience demo / future TTT-style multi-tab apps).

api.sig: pub fn respond(self: Request, status: integer, body: text)
pub fn respond_typed(self: Request, status: integer, body: text, content_type: text)
pub fn respond_html(self: Request, body: text)
api.doc: Convenience wrappers for the common cases TTT v3 needs.

api.sig: pub fn respond_css(self: Request, body: text)
pub fn respond_js(self: Request, body: text)
pub fn respond_loft(self: Request, body: text)
pub fn send(self: WebSocket, msg: text) -> boolean
pub fn send_binary(self: WebSocket, msg: text) -> boolean
api.doc:
Send `msg` as a binary WebSocket frame (opcode 2).  Loft
`text` is a byte buffer — pack u8/u16/i32/etc. into it via
`to_bytes` style code, then ship it.

Opcode of the last frame returned by `next` on this socket.
Use after `next` to decide whether to interpret the payload
as utf-8 text or as a binary blob.

`message` are mutually-exclusive booleans for fast dispatch
without an integer enum.  Wire format `<msg_id>:<payload>`
is parsed by `run` so msg_id and payload come pre-split.

api.sig: pub fn run(self: Server, on_event: fn(WsEvent))
api.doc:
Block forever, draining events and calling the handler.
Malformed message frames are logged at warn level and silently
dropped — the handler never sees them.

Non-blocking single-event drain — returns the next WsEvent, or null
when nothing is pending.  Lets a server that also needs a periodic
tick (timers, decay, snapshots) run its OWN loop while still using the
efficient Rust-side event pump: drain `poll_event` in a batch, do the
periodic work, idle-sleep when a pass produced nothing.  `run` above is
the convenience wrapper for servers that only react to events.

Disconnect (kind 2) and malformed message frames are skipped internally
(the caller never sees them), so a null return always means "idle".

api.sig: pub fn respond_html(self: Server, body: text)
api.doc:
Reply to the HTTP request currently parked by a `http` WsEvent and
close it.  Call exactly once from the handler for an `ev.http` event.

api.sig: pub fn respond_typed(self: Server, status: integer, body: text, content_type: text)
pub fn send_to(self: Server, client_id: integer, msg: text) -> boolean
api.doc: Send a frame to a specific client.  Returns true on success.

api.sig: pub fn broadcast(self: Server, msg: text) -> integer
api.doc:
Send a frame to every active client.  Returns the count of
successful sends.  Iteration happens entirely in Rust.

api.sig: pub fn disconnect(self: Server, client_id: integer)
api.doc:
Forcefully close one client's connection.  The slot is freed
for reuse by future accepts.

game_protocol.description: Packet framing + ack/retransmit for loft game-networking demos.

random.description: Pseudo-random number generation — PCG-64 thread-local PRNG with rand_seed reproducibility.

regex.description: Small-script regex: matches / find / split / match_groups / replace, with a thread-local pattern cache.

time.description: Date/time arithmetic on millisecond-since-epoch integers — proleptic Gregorian, same model as JavaScript Date.  Pure-loft; identical results on interp, --native, WASM.

hex_grid.description: Hex-grid geometry — axial/pixel conversion, neighbours, distance, and corner offsets.

hex_terrain.description: The OVERLAND terrain layer of the `hex_*` family: a coarse overland hex lattice

hex_world.description: Sparse 32x32-chunk hex-grid world data model — Cell / Chunk / World with binary save/load.

api.doc:
4-byte cell.  c_color uses the u8 typedef (integer limit(0,255)
size(1)); c_age uses u16 (size(2)).  Storage packs to exactly 4
bytes per cell — the projector ships them through the binary
surface in chunk-major order.

c_color = 0 means "empty" (no crystal, no TTT mark).  Both clients
treat 0 as background; non-zero as live content.

32x32 grid at chunk coords (ck_cx, ck_cz).  Index into ck_cells:
hx * 32 + hz where hx, hz ∈ [0, 31].  Same shape as moros_map's
Chunk minus the cy layer (this world is single-layer).

Top-level container.  Sparse: only chunks with at least one
non-empty cell live in `chunks`; empty chunks are removed by the
tick loop's GC pass.  `tick` is monotonic per server lifetime.

floor(v / 32) — handles negative v correctly (C-style `/` rounds
toward zero, which silently maps cell (-1, 0) to chunk 0 instead
of chunk -1).
v mod 32, always in [0, 31].

Default empty cell.  All fields zero; serialises to four 0x00
bytes on the wire.

api.sig: pub fn build_chunk(cx: integer, cz: integer) -> Chunk
api.doc:
Build a 32x32 chunk filled with empty cells at chunk coords
(cx, cz).
New empty world, tick 0, no chunks.

api.sig: pub fn has_chunk(self: World, q: integer, r: integer) -> boolean
api.doc: True iff a chunk exists at the global (q, r).

api.sig: pub fn ensure_chunk(self: &World, q: integer, r: integer)
api.doc:
Ensure a chunk exists at the global (q, r); creates a 32x32 of
empty cells if absent.

api.sig: pub fn get_cell(self: World, q: integer, r: integer) -> Cell
api.doc:
Read the cell at global (q, r).  Returns `cell_empty()` when the
chunk does not exist (sparse-world convention — empty chunks are
implicitly all-empty).

api.sig: pub fn set_cell(self: &World, q: integer, r: integer, value: Cell)
api.doc:
Write a cell at global (q, r).  Creates the containing chunk on
demand; does NOT GC empty chunks (the tick loop's end-of-tick
pass owns that).
Total number of non-empty cells across every chunk.

api.sig: pub fn neighbour_count(self: World, q: integer, r: integer) -> integer
api.doc:
Count filled axial-hex neighbours of the cell at global (q, r).
Returns 0..=6.  Empty centre cell is fine — neighbours are read
independently of the centre's state.

── Tick + decay ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

One world step: advances `tick`, ages every non-empty cell by 1,
then removes any cell whose `c_age` has crossed
`effective_lifetime + decay_window` where
`effective_lifetime = base_lifetime + neighbour_lease *
filled_neighbour_count`.  Neighbour counts are computed against
the PRE-TICK state — a single tick can remove a connected blob
of cells, but the cascade (centre losing its neighbour support)
only fires on the NEXT tick.  This keeps the simulation
deterministic per-step and prevents one-tick complete collapse
of large clusters.

api.sig: pub fn world_save(self: World, path: text)
api.doc: Save the world to `path`, replacing whatever was there.

api.sig: pub fn world_load(self: &World, path: text) -> integer
api.doc:
Load the world from `path`.  Mutates `self` in place; pre-existing
cells in `self` are left untouched unless the snapshot overwrites
the same (q, r).  Returns the number of cells loaded.  Returns 0 if
the file does not exist, has the wrong magic, or carries an
unsupported version — never panics.

input.description: Game input for loft — action/axis bindings resolved to per-tick input state.

markdown.description: Markdown-to-HTML rendering for loft — headings, inline spans, slugify, link rewriting.
